title: VoroNav Voronoi-based Zero-shot Object Navigation with Large Language Model
abstract: In the realm of household robotics, the Zero-Shot Object Navigation (ZSON) task empowers agents to adeptly traverse unfamiliar environments and locate objects from novel categories without prior explicit training. This paper introduces VoroNav, a novel semantic exploration framework that proposes the Reduced Voronoi Graph to extract exploratory paths and planning nodes from a semantic map constructed in real time. By harnessing topological and semantic information, VoroNav designs text-based descriptions of paths and images that are readily interpretable by a large language model (LLM). In particular, our approach presents a synergy of path and farsight descriptions to represent the environmental context, enabling LLM to apply commonsense reasoning to ascertain waypoints for navigation. Extensive evaluation on HM3D and HSSD validates VoroNav surpasses existing benchmarks in both success rate and exploration efficiency (absolute improvement: +2.8% Success and +3.7% SPL on HM3D, +2.6% Success and +3.8% SPL on HSSD). Additionally introduced metrics that evaluate obstacle avoidance proficiency and perceptual efficiency further corroborate the enhancements achieved by our method in ZSON planning. Project page: https://voro-nav.github.io
impact: VoroNav addresses the challenges of autonomous navigation in robotics, holding significant implications for the future of household robotics. By leveraging the synergy of semantic mapping and LLMs, it promises enhanced efficiency and effectiveness in robotic navigation tasks. Ethically, VoroNav represents a step forward in responsible AI application, balancing technological advancement with the need for safe, reliable, and intuitive robot behavior in domestic settings. To address safety and ethical concerns, our experiments are conducted in controlled, simulated environments using opensource datasets. This approach ensures the predictability and safety of the agent’s behaviors. However, the reliability of generative models in practical applications remains an area for future research. Key to this advancement is improving the accuracy and precision of LLMs in planning and action prediction, which is crucial for ensuring safety in real-world deployment.

query_input.scenario: Warehouse inventory robot navigating to collect stock
query_input.issue: Incorrect path descriptors derived from Voronoi graph exploration lead to inefficient routing or mission failure.
hazard: LLM-generated semantic maps and path descriptors from Voronoi graph exploration may produce erroneous environmental representations, causing navigation failures.
exposure: Warehouse workers and automated inventory systems relying on robot couriers.

hipporag_result.vulnerability: Warehouse operations' dependence on autonomous robot couriers without human oversight or fallback verification of LLM-generated path descriptors.
hipporag_result.impact: Delayed or failed stock retrieval disrupting inventory workflows and reducing warehouse throughput.
hipporag_result.dose_response: Erroneous LLM-generated semantic maps and path descriptors, through warehouse workers and automated inventory systems relying on robot couriers, trigger dependence on autonomous navigation without human oversight, leading to delayed stock retrieval disrupting workflows and reducing throughput.

dose_response: LLM-generated erroneous path descriptors cause routing errors that, accepted without validation by automated logistics, propagate, disrupting order fulfillment and causing supply chain delays.
vulnerability: Automated logistics systems accept LLM path plans without real-time validation against physical layout, allowing routing errors to propagate.
impact: Mission failures disrupt order fulfillment, causing supply chain delays and increased operational costs.
